title: Optimization Landscape and Two-Layer Neural Networks
speaker: Rong Ge
affiliation: Duke University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Modern machine learning often optimizes a nonconvex objective using simple algorithm such as gradient descent. One way of explaining the success of such simple algorithms is by analyzing the optimization landscape and show that all local minima are also globally optimal. However, even for two-layer neural networks, the optimization landscape is hard to analyze and have many different regimes, depending on the size of a student network compared to the teacher network or the size of training set.

proof_stages.label: Symmetry obstructions to convex landscapes
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates landscape analysis as the missing explanation for simple gradient methods and shows that parameter symmetries in multi-component models make natural objectives nonconvex and saddle-rich.

proof_stages.label: Locally optimizable template and neural-network limitations
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines locally optimizable functions as having only symmetric global local minima and only strict saddles, recalls successes in low-rank settings, and then explains why standard two-layer neural-network losses violate this hope and motivate exact-parameter and mildly overparameterized alternatives.

proof_stages.label: Symmetric-input one-ReLU moment identity
proof_stages.summary: For the first result, the input law is assumed symmetric and a one-neuron warm-up proves the identity E[yx]=1/2 E[xx^T]w, yielding a method-of-moments recovery algorithm for a single ReLU neuron.

proof_stages.label: Pure-neuron detector for hidden-layer inversion
proof_stages.summary: A multi-output two-layer network is reduced to one-neuron problems by seeking directions u for which u^T A is a basis vector, and a higher-order moment equation is built whose vanishing detects exactly such pure neurons under general-position assumptions.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial-time recovery via linearized equations
proof_stages.summary: The detector is estimated from samples, its quadratic system is solved by linearization and simultaneous diagonalization, and each recovered direction is fed into the one-neuron estimator, with empirical behavior and limitations discussed.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic-activation interpolation landscape
proof_stages.summary: For the memorization result, quadratic activations show that about d neurons can fit about d^2 general-position samples, and when r>2d+2 all local minima of the squared loss interpolate the data.

proof_stages.label: Random polynomial lifting for mildly overparameterized memorization
proof_stages.summary: A fixed random polynomial first layer maps n samples to a roughly sqrt(n)-dimensional well-conditioned representation so the quadratic-layer landscape theorem gives gradient-descent interpolation with far fewer neurons than NTK, followed by empirical checks and open problems.

examples.name: Permutation symmetry in k-center clustering
examples.note: The k-center clustering picture shows that permuting indistinguishable components gives multiple equivalent optima whose convex average is not a valid optimum, producing the idea that symmetry obstructs convex reformulations.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the k-center solution, permutes centers, and uses the convex combination to show why a convex objective would create a bad two-center-like solution.

passes.reason: Unordered k-centers have full label-permutation symmetry and are used to obstruct convexity via permuted optima.

The full center-label permutation symmetry is used to force a convex midpoint where two centers collapse, obstructing convexity.

examples.name: Sign-flip symmetric polynomial objective
examples.note: The even-power polynomial objective illustrates how sign symmetries create several equivalent minima and force saddle points along suitable connecting paths, motivating the strict-saddle part of the landscape template.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the sign-flip symmetry of the polynomial objective, identifies the four symmetric minima, and reasons that saddle points arise along paths between them.

examples.name: Gaussian teacher network with bad local minima
examples.note: A simple two-layer teacher network with Gaussian inputs and orthogonal hidden weights is used as a counterexample showing that standard neural-network losses need not be locally optimizable, forcing the talk toward other algorithms or overparameterization.
examples.engagement_reason: The Gaussian teacher-network example is cited as a known setting with bad local minima, with facts stated but no derivation or construction carried out.

examples.name: MNIST and PCA-MNIST memorization experiments
examples.note: Experiments on MNIST and on its top PCA directions illustrate the interpolation theorem and suggest that similar-sized ReLU networks also memorize despite the absence of a proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes MNIST and PCA-MNIST memorization experiments, including loss curves, comparison with ReLU networks, and observed generalization behavior.

passes.reason: The experiments place small near-parameter-counting networks at the interpolation boundary by achieving zero training loss.
